title: The end of the Cold War
description: Test your skills on the causes and effects of the end of the Cold War!
topic_path: The end of the Cold War | 9.3
The end of the Cold War

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the following question.

> “In an ironic sense Karl Marx was right. We are witnessing today a great revolutionary crisis, a crisis where the demands of the economic order are conflicting directly with those of the political order. But the crisis is happening not in the free, non-Marxist West but in the home of Marxism-Leninism, the Soviet Union. It is the Soviet Union that runs against the tide of history by denying human freedom and human dignity to its citizens.”

>-Source: President Reagan, Speech to the House of Commons, 1982

**Which of the following issues of the period was Reagan most likely concerned with in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the influence of communism
the fall of the Soviet Union
the rise of labor unions
the start of a recession

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the following question.

> “In an ironic sense Karl Marx was right. We are witnessing today a great revolutionary crisis, a crisis where the demands of the economic order are conflicting directly with those of the political order. But the crisis is happening not in the free, non-Marxist West but in the home of Marxism-Leninism, the Soviet Union. It is the Soviet Union that runs against the tide of history by denying human freedom and human dignity to its citizens.”

>-Source: President Reagan, Speech to the House of Commons, 1982

**Ideas similar to those expressed in the passage have directly contributed to which of the following US actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the allocation of funds to foreign forces fighting communist governments in their countries
the use of nuclear warfare to target communist countries before the outbreak of a war
the establishment of a collective security organization with other democratic countries
the declaration of war on the Soviet Union to protect susceptible countries from communism

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the following question.

> “Moscow immediately noted Reagan’s pledge to assist anti-Soviet rebels in the Third World. The Soviet leaders countered the US initiative by prodding the radical governments in Angola, Cambodia, Nicaragua and Afghanistan to launch military offensives against the US-supported rebels. . . . 
> “Committed to the orthodox tit-for-tat logic of the Cold War, the Reagan administration responded by upping the ante. It sought once more to augment US assistance to the Angolan, Cambodian, Nicaraguan and Afghan rebel movements. The proponents of the Reagan Doctrine began to pressure more moderate members and groups in the administration to supply the rebels with more money and better arms.”

>-Source: Torbjorn L. Knutsen, historian, “The Reagan Doctrine and the Lessons from the Afghan War,”  1992

**The excerpt best illustrates which of the following developments in the 1980s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the use of limited military intervention to oppose communism
the start of a series of proxy wars between the United States and the Soviet Union
the influence of the Monroe Doctrine on foreign policy
the de-escalation of the Cold War after strategic arms limitation talks

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the following question.

> “Moscow immediately noted Reagan’s pledge to assist anti-Soviet rebels in the Third World. The Soviet leaders countered the US initiative by prodding the radical governments in Angola, Cambodia, Nicaragua and Afghanistan to launch military offensives against the US-supported rebels. . . . 
> “Committed to the orthodox tit-for-tat logic of the Cold War, the Reagan administration responded by upping the ante. It sought once more to augment US assistance to the Angolan, Cambodian, Nicaraguan and Afghan rebel movements. The proponents of the Reagan Doctrine began to pressure more moderate members and groups in the administration to supply the rebels with more money and better arms.”

>-Source: Torbjorn L. Knutsen, historian, “The Reagan Doctrine and the Lessons from the Afghan War,”  1992

**Which of the following earlier US actions was most similar to the actions described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the support offered by the federal government under the Marshall Plan
the dedication to détente and peace during the Nixon administration
the commitment to isolationism after involvement in World War I
the declaration that the Western Hemisphere was off-limits to European powers

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the following question.

> “The defense policy of the United States is based on a simple premise: The United States does not start fights. We will never be an aggressor. We maintain our strength in order to deter and defend against aggression — to preserve freedom and peace.

> “Since the dawn of the atomic age, we've sought to reduce the risk of war by maintaining a strong deterrent and by seeking genuine arms control. ‘Deterrence’ means simply this: making sure any adversary who thinks about attacking the United States, or our allies, or our vital interests, concludes that the risks to him outweigh any potential gains. Once he understands that, he won't attack. We maintain the peace through our strength; weakness only invites aggression.”

>-Source: President Ronald Reagan, “Address to the Nation on Defense and National Security,” 1983

**Which of the following earlier trends emerged from sentiments similar to those Reagan expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: an arms race in the 1950s
imperialism in the 1890s
isolationism in the 1920s
labor-saving technology in the 1870s

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the following question.

> “The defense policy of the United States is based on a simple premise: The United States does not start fights. We will never be an aggressor. We maintain our strength in order to deter and defend against aggression — to preserve freedom and peace.

> “Since the dawn of the atomic age, we've sought to reduce the risk of war by maintaining a strong deterrent and by seeking genuine arms control. ‘Deterrence’ means simply this: making sure any adversary who thinks about attacking the United States, or our allies, or our vital interests, concludes that the risks to him outweigh any potential gains. Once he understands that, he won't attack. We maintain the peace through our strength; weakness only invites aggression.”

>-Source: President Ronald Reagan, “Address to the Nation on Defense and National Security,” 1983

**Reagan’s account in the excerpt above most directly encouraged which of the following changes in subsequent years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the buildup of nuclear and conventional weapons
the establishment of the United Nations
the declaration of war on the Soviet Union
the initiation of Strategic Arms Limitation Talks

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f51078d55f82d74d5903e119372f915.svg)

**Which of the following statements best explains the change over time in military spending from 1980 to 1990 for the United States depicted in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Reagan administration believed in “peace through strength” and undertook an arms buildup.
The Reagan administration orchestrated a series of arms limitation talks to promote peace.
The Reagan administration dealt with an economic recession and had to reduce military spending.
The Reagan administration committed to getting a rocket to Mars, increasing NASA’s budget.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f51078d55f82d74d5903e119372f915.svg)

**Which of the following was a significant cause of the trend from 1970 to 1990 shown in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: involvement in the Cold War
start of the War on Terror
end of the Vietnam War
creation of the GI Bill

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f51078d55f82d74d5903e119372f915.svg)

**The graph most strongly supports which of the following arguments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increased US military spending was a significant factor that brought about the end of the Cold War.
The United States and the Soviet Union reduced military spending after negotiations that aimed to limit nuclear warhead production.
Increased US military spending helped the economy after a period of stagflation in the 1970s.
The Soviet Union’s increased military spending forced the United States to surrender during the Cold War out of fear of total annihilation.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Having won the Cold War, it was by no means inevitable that the US would maintain its global engagement. Some argued that the US, and others had paid a high price for American involvement around the world. It would be prudent to retreat if not into isolationism then to a more modest role. These voices struggled for a hearing in both parties. Those that did argue for a change, as Pat Buchanan, were roundly defeated at the ballot box. Although the Cold War consensus fueled by the Soviet threat was not replaced by a comparable policy or ideological framework, the majority view was that America had widespread global interests and the US should maintain its global engagement. The mainstream debate after the Cold War was thus limited to the size and nature of American engagement, not the engagement itself.” 

>-Source: Fraser Cameron, political scientist, *US Foreign Policy After the Cold War: Global Hegemon or Reluctant Sheriff?* 2006

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that the end of the Cold War had on members of the American public?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They began to debate the appropriate level of American involvement in the affairs of other nations.
They called on the federal government to return to isolationism and end international military engagements.
They wanted to reduce military spending and allocate more money to solving social issues.
They argued that the US government should reduce its role in the United Nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Having won the Cold War, it was by no means inevitable that the US would maintain its global engagement. Some argued that the US, and others had paid a high price for American involvement around the world. It would be prudent to retreat if not into isolationism then to a more modest role. These voices struggled for a hearing in both parties. Those that did argue for a change, as Pat Buchanan, were roundly defeated at the ballot box. Although the Cold War consensus fueled by the Soviet threat was not replaced by a comparable policy or ideological framework, the majority view was that America had widespread global interests and the US should maintain its global engagement. The mainstream debate after the Cold War was thus limited to the size and nature of American engagement, not the engagement itself.” 

>-Source: Fraser Cameron, political scientist, *US Foreign Policy After the Cold War: Global Hegemon or Reluctant Sheriff?* 2006

**Which of the following earlier developments had an effect most similar to that described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Allied victory in World War II in the 1940s
the Red Scare after World War I in the 1920s
the era of reform after the Market Revolution in the 1830s
the era of nativist sentiment in the 1890s

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Having won the Cold War, it was by no means inevitable that the US would maintain its global engagement. Some argued that the US, and others had paid a high price for American involvement around the world. It would be prudent to retreat if not into isolationism then to a more modest role. These voices struggled for a hearing in both parties. Those that did argue for a change, as Pat Buchanan, were roundly defeated at the ballot box. Although the Cold War consensus fueled by the Soviet threat was not replaced by a comparable policy or ideological framework, the majority view was that America had widespread global interests and the US should maintain its global engagement. The mainstream debate after the Cold War was thus limited to the size and nature of American engagement, not the engagement itself.” 

>-Source: Fraser Cameron, political scientist, *US Foreign Policy After the Cold War: Global Hegemon or Reluctant Sheriff?* 2006

**Which of the following is one important continuity in American foreign policy throughout the late twentieth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States had a significant influence on global politics and remained a superpower.
The United States retreated from global conflicts and returned to a policy of isolationism.
The United States refused to cultivate peacetime relationships with other democratic countries.
The United States developed free trade agreements with several countries to improve the American economy.
